title: Chocolate éclairs
subtitle: Choux fingers filled with vanilla crème pâtissière and dipped in chocolate ganache
excerpt: Twelve chocolate éclairs: a choux paste piped into 12 cm fingers, baked until hollow, filled with vanilla crème pâtissière, and finished with a dark chocolate ganache glaze. The choux is cooked twice (first on the hob, then in the oven) and the filling is made from scratch.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the cream pastry section documents choux paste made by boiling water and butter, beating flour into the hot liquid until the paste forms a smooth ball, then incorporating eggs one at a time until glossy. The technique of filling baked choux shells with crème pâtissière and finishing with chocolate is documented in British domestic cookery from the Edwardian period onward as éclairs became a standard patisserie form. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the pastry cream section documents the custard base used to fill baked pastry shells.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unfilled, baked choux shells in a sealed bag. Re-crisp in a 180°C fan oven for 8 minutes before filling and glazing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The crème pâtissière keeps refrigerated for 2 days, covered directly with cling film. Fill and glaze éclairs no more than 2 hours before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, top third; reduce to 170°C fan after 15 minutes to dry the shells

recipeTools.notes: For loosening the cold crème pâtissière before piping.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked paste made by boiling water and butter, beating in flour to form a smooth ball that pulls away from the pan, then beating in eggs until the paste is glossy and falls from the spoon in a thick, slow V-shape. The flour is cooked on the hob before the eggs are added, gelatinising the starch so the paste holds its shape when piped. In the oven, moisture from the eggs turns to steam and inflates the shells hollow.

content.text: Choux pastry is unlike any other. The paste is cooked twice: first on the hob, where the flour gelatinises in the hot butter and water; then in the oven, where the moisture in the eggs turns to steam and inflates the shell hollow. The result is a crisp, airy finger with almost nothing inside. That cavity holds the filling.
The critical moment is adding the eggs to the

. Add one portion at a time, beating fully after each addition. Stop when the paste falls from a lifted spoon in a thick, slow V-shape and looks glossy. Too much egg and the paste is too slack to hold its shape; too little and the shells will not hollow out.

attrs.title: Keep the oven door shut
attrs.body: Do not open the oven for the first 20 minutes. Choux shells inflate from steam pressure inside. Opening the door too early drops the temperature, the shells collapse, and they will not reinflate. Once deep golden, prop the door open 1 cm with a wooden spoon for the final 5 minutes to drive out residual moisture.

items.prepNote: large, at room temperature, beaten together

content.text: The custard needs time to cool and set, so make it before the choux. Bring the {{whole-milk}} milk to a simmer in a saucepan. In a bowl, whisk the {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{cornflour}} together until smooth and pale. Pour the hot milk slowly over the yolk mixture in a thin stream, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and cook over medium heat, stirring with a spatula, until the custard thickens, comes to a boil, and large bubbles pop at the surface, about 3 minutes. Remove from the heat. Stir in the {{vanilla-extract}} and {{unsalted-butter}} butter. Press cling film directly onto the surface to prevent a skin forming. Refrigerate until cold and firm, at least 1 hour.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Put the {{water}}, {{whole-milk}} milk, {{unsalted-butter}} butter cubes, and {{sea-salt-fine}} salt into a medium saucepan. Bring to a full boil. The moment it boils, remove from the heat and tip in all the {{plain-flour}} sifted flour at once. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the dough comes together into a smooth ball and pulls cleanly away from the pan sides. Return to low heat and stir for 1–2 minutes to dry the paste slightly. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes — the paste should be warm but not hot enough to scramble the eggs.

Add the {{eggs}} beaten egg to the paste a tablespoon at a time, beating thoroughly after each addition. The paste will look curdled and lumpy at first — keep beating, it will smooth out. Stop adding egg when the paste is smooth, glossy, and falls from a lifted spoon in a thick, slow V-shape. You may not need all the egg.

Transfer the paste to a piping bag fitted with a plain 1 cm round nozzle. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Pipe 12 fingers about 12 cm long, leaving 4 cm between each. Dampen a fingertip and smooth down any points at the ends. Bake at 200°C fan for 15 minutes, then reduce to 170°C fan and bake for a further 20 minutes until deep golden and completely firm when pressed. Pierce each éclair once at the end with a skewer to release steam. Transfer to a rack and cool completely before filling.

Bring the {{double-cream}} cream to a simmer in a small saucepan. Pour over the {{dark-chocolate}} chopped chocolate in a heatproof bowl. Leave for 2 minutes, then stir from the centre outward until the ganache is smooth and glossy. Cool until it has thickened slightly but is still fluid enough to dip into, about 15 minutes.

Beat the cold crème pâtissière briefly with a spoon to loosen it. Fill a piping bag and pipe the cream into each éclair through the steam hole at one end, or cut the top off and spoon it in. Dip each filled éclair top-side down into the ganache, letting the excess drip back into the bowl. Set on the rack. Serve within 2 hours of filling — the shells soften against the cream as they sit.
